statement.title: Statement
statement.content:
Description
This is the hard version of the problem. The difference between the versions is that in this version $ a_i \leq n $ .

You are given an array of $ n $ integers $ a_1, a_2, \dots, a_n $ .

Your task is to find a subarray $ a[l, r] $ (a continuous sequence of elements $ a_l, a_{l + 1}, \dots, a_r $ ) for which the value of the expression $ \text{med}(a[l, r]) - \min(a[l, r]) $ is maximized.

Here:

- $ \text{med} $ is the median of the subarray, that is, the element at position $ \left\lceil \frac{k + 1}{2} \right\rceil $ after sorting the subarray, where $ k $ is its length;
- $ \min $ is the minimum element of this subarray.

For example, consider the array $ a=[1, 4, 1, 5, 3, 3] $ and choose the subarray $ a[2, 5] = [4, 1, 5, 3] $ . In sorted form, it looks like $ [1, 3, 4, 5] $ .

- $ \text{med}(a[2, 5]) = 4 $ , since $ \left\lceil \frac{4 + 1}{2} \right\rceil = $ the third element in the sorted subarray is $ 4 $ ;
- $ \min(a[2, 5]) = 1 $ , since the minimum element is $ 1 $ .

In this example, the value $ \text{med} - \min = 4 - 1 = 3 $ .

Input Format
The first line contains an integer $ t $ ( $ 1 \le t \le 10^4 $ ) — the number of test cases.

The first line of each test case contains one integer $ n $ ( $ 1 \leq n \leq 2 \cdot 10^5 $ ) — the length of the array.

The second line of each test case contains $ n $ integers $ a_1, a_2, \dots, a_n $ ( $ 1 \leq a_i \leq n $ ) — the elements of the array.

It is guaranteed that the sum of $ n $ across all test cases does not exceed $ 2 \cdot 10^5 $ .

Output Format
For each test case, output one integer — the maximum possible value of $ \text{med} - \min $ among all subarrays of the array.

Examples
Sample 1
Input
5
5
3 2 5 3 1
4
4 1 1 3
7
6 1 3 4 6 2 7
4
4 2 3 1
5
1 2 3 4 5
Output
3
3
5
2
2

Note
In the first example, consider the array: $ a=[3,\ 2,\ 5,\ 3,\ 1] $ you can choose the subarray $ a[2,\ 3] $ , that is, the elements $ [2,\ 5] $ .

- The length of the subarray is $ 2 $ .
- The median is the element at position $ \left\lceil \dfrac{3}{2} \right\rceil = 2 $ in the sorted subarray. After sorting, we get $ [2,\ 5] $ , $ \text{med} = 5 $ .
- The minimum element of the subarray: $ \min = 2 $ .

Therefore, $ \text{med} - \min = 5 - 2 = 3 $ , which is the maximum answer.

In the second test, the array: $ a=[4,\ 1,\ 1,\ 3] $ you can choose the subarray $ a[1,\ 2] $ , that is, the elements $ [4,\ 1] $ .

- The length of the subarray is $ 2 $ .
- The median is the element at position $ \left\lceil \dfrac{3}{2} \right\rceil = 2 $ in the sorted subarray. After sorting, we get $ [1,\ 4] $ , $ \text{med} = 4 $ .
- The minimum element of the subarray: $ \min = 1 $ .

Therefore, $ \text{med} - \min = 4 - 1 = 3 $ .

It can be proven that both of these subarrays are optimal and yield the maximum value of the expression $ \text{med} - \min $ .

solutions.title: Tutorial
solutions.content:
2126G2 - Big Wins! (hard version)
Goal: maximise  $$$median(subarray) − minimum(subarray)$$$. Treat the minimum as the "left pointer" $$$mn$$$ that we increase from $$$1$$$ up to max $$$a_i$$$. For every fixed $$$mn$$$ we try to push a second pointer $$$med$$$ (the candidate median) as high as possible while some subarray exists whose minimum is exactly $$$mn$$$ and whose median is at least $$$med$$$. The answer is the largest difference $$$med − mn$$$ reached during this sweep.
Fix a value $$$mn$$$. Any subarray whose minimum equals $$$mn$$$ must contain at least one position $$$u$$$ with $$$a_u = mn$$$, and the whole subarray must lie inside the maximal span where that $$$u$$$ is the strict minimum. Those spans are computed once by the usual monotone stack: $$$l_u =$$$ first index to the left that has $$$value  \lt  a[u] + 1$$$, $$$r_u = $$$first index to the right that has $$$value  \lt  a_u - 1$$$.
For a potential median $$$M$$$ define an array $$$sign_i$$$ = $$$+1$$$ if $$$a_i ≥ M$$$, $$$−1$$$ otherwise. Inside any interval its median is $$$≥ M$$$ exactly when the total sum of sign over that interval is positive (at least half the elements are high). Therefore, for a particular position $$$u$$$ to serve as the minimum we need
$$$maxSuffix(l_u, u−1) + sign_u + maxPrefix(u+1, r_u) ≥ 0$$$.
Here $$$maxSuffix$$$ and $$$maxPrefix$$$ are, respectively, the largest suffix sum ending at $$$u−1$$$ and the largest prefix sum starting at $$$u+1$$$, measured in the sign array.
All max-prefix / max-suffix / total-sum queries can be answered in $$$O(log n)$$$ with a segment-tree that stores at each node ($$$ans, bestPrefix, bestSuffix, totalSum$$$). Merging two children follows the standard Kadane-style formulas.
Algorithm loop. Initialise the segment tree with $$$sign ≡ +1$$$, so total sum everywhere is positive; set $$$med = 1$$$. For $$$med←1$$$ we must exclude from the tree every position whose value is $$$ \lt  med$$$, by updating that point to $$$−1$$$. Now iterate $$$mn$$$ from $$$1$$$ upward:
- for every index $$$u$$$ with $$$a_u = mn$$$ evaluate the inequality above.
- while the inequality is satisfied for all such indices, increment $$$med$$$ by $$$1$$$ and for every position whose value just became $$$ \lt  med$$$ perform one point-update to $$$−1$$$ in the segment tree.
- when the test fails for some $$$u$$$, stop raising med.
- record $$$ans = max(ans, med − mn)$$$.
Because med only increases and never exceeds $$$n$$$, the total number of tree updates is $$$O(n)$$$ and every update / query costs $$$O(log n)$$$, so the whole run is $$$O(n log n)$$$. Memory is $$$O(n)$$$.
Why correct. For each mn the inner while moves med upward greedily until the moment no subarray with minimum mn can keep the $$$median ≥ med$$$; thus the last valid value of $$$med$$$ realises the true maximum for this $$$mn$$$. The outer sweep examines every possible minimum, hence the largest gap ever recorded is the global optimum.
